slides.heading:
Humanoid Robots
Enter the Workforce
slides.body: From factory floors to warehouses — the age of robot coworkers is here

slides.heading: What Are Humanoid Robots?
slides.body: Humanoid robots are bipedal machines designed to move and interact like humans. Unlike traditional industrial arms, they can navigate real-world spaces, use human tools, and adapt to unstructured environments.

slides.heading: The Breakthrough: AI-Powered Movement
slides.body: Modern humanoids use deep reinforcement learning to master balance, grasping, and locomotion. Training in simulation — then transferring skills to the real world — has cut development time from years to months.

slides.heading: Who's Leading the Race?
slides.body: Tesla's Optimus, Figure AI, Boston Dynamics Atlas, and Agility Robotics' Digit are frontrunners. Chinese firms like Unitree and Fourier Intelligence are also rapidly scaling, creating intense global competition.

slides.heading: Where They're Being Deployed First
slides.body: Automotive factories, e-commerce fulfillment centers, and logistics warehouses are the first deployment zones. BMW, Amazon, and GXO Logistics are already piloting humanoid robots in live operations.

slides.heading: The Economics of Robot Labor
slides.body: Early units cost $50,000–$200,000, but prices are projected to fall below $20,000 by 2030. At that price point, robots could outcompete human labor in many repetitive physical tasks within a decade.

slides.heading: Human-Robot Collaboration
slides.body: Rather than full replacement, most deployments use robots alongside humans. Robots handle heavy lifting and monotonous tasks while humans oversee, troubleshoot, and manage exceptions — raising overall productivity.

slides.heading: Safety Challenges Still to Solve
slides.body: Humanoids operating near people require rigorous safety protocols. Force-limiting sensors, real-time collision detection, and regulatory certification frameworks are still maturing as deployment scales up.

slides.heading: Jobs at Risk — and Jobs Created
slides.body: Roles involving repetitive physical labor face the most disruption. At the same time, new jobs are emerging in robot fleet management, teleoperation, maintenance, and AI training — a shift, not just a loss.

slides.heading: The Robot Coworker Era Has Begun
slides.body: Humanoid robots won't replace human ingenuity — but they will reshape every physical industry. Understanding this shift now is the best preparation for the workforce of tomorrow.
